coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) possess unique optical properties, showing great potential for applications in optical imaging, detection and light-emitting diodes (LEDs). However, traditional synthetic methods usually adopt intense reaction, including hydrothermal and solvothermal methods, which inhibited the production and application of CDs. In this work, CDs were prepared via an electrochemical method utilizing graphite as the electrode and 1-butyl-3-methylimidazole chloride salt ([BMIM]Cl), an ionic liquid, as the electrolyte. This method is safe, controllable, and easy to operate under mild conditions. Three CDs with sizes of 2.98, 3.43, and 5.92 nm were synthesized by varying electrode materials (graphite and Pt wire). HRTEM images showed crystalline graphite structures with clear two-dimensional lattice fringes. Their chemical composition was further analyzed using XPS, FT-IR, and Raman spectroscopy. Their optical properties were evaluated through fluorescence, UV-Vis, and decay curves. CDs prepared with graphite as both electrodes showed a redshifted optimal excitation/emission wavelength (390/475 nm) compared to those with graphite as the anode and Pt as the cathode (320/425 nm). Furthermore, the luminescence and formation of these CDs were revealed. At last, these CDs were successfully applied in LEDs. This electrochemical approach provides a promising foundation for the preparation of CDs and their subsequent applications in LEDs.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) possess unique optical properties, showing great potential for applications in optical imaging, detection and light-emitting diodes (LEDs). However, traditional synthetic methods usually adopt intense reaction, including hydrothermal and solvothermal methods, which inhibited the production and application of CDs. In this work, CDs were prepared via an electrochemical method utilizing graphite as the electrode and 1-butyl-3-methylimidazole chloride salt ([BMIM]Cl), an ionic liquid, as the electrolyte. This method is safe, controllable, and easy to operate under mild conditions. Three CDs with sizes of 2.98, 3.43, and 5.92 nm were synthesized by varying electrode materials (graphite and Pt wire). HRTEM images showed crystalline graphite structures with clear two-dimensional lattice fringes. Their chemical composition was further analyzed using XPS, FT-IR, and Raman spectroscopy. Their optical properties were evaluated through fluorescence, UV-Vis, and decay curves. CDs prepared with graphite as both electrodes showed a redshifted optimal excitation/emission wavelength (390/475 nm) compared to those with graphite as the anode and Pt as the cathode (320/425 nm). Furthermore, the luminescence and formation of these CDs were revealed. At last, these CDs were successfully applied in LEDs. This electrochemical approach provides a promising foundation for the preparation of CDs and their subsequent applications in LEDs.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) possess unique optical properties, showing great potential for applications in optical imaging, detection and light-emitting diodes (LEDs). However, traditional synthetic methods usually adopt intense reaction, which inhibited the production and application of CDs. In this work, we present a safe, controllable and straightforward electrochemical method for the synthesizing CDs. Three CDs with sizes of 2.98, 3.43, and 5.92 nm were synthesized by varying electrode materials (graphite and Pt wire). These CDs possess crystalline graphite structures with distinct two-dimensional lattice fringes and exhibit surfaces rich in oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functional groups. Notably, CDs prepared with graphite as both electrodes showed a redshifted optimal excitation/emission wavelength (390/475 nm) compared to those with graphite as the anode and Pt as the cathode (320/425 nm). Furthermore, the luminescence and formation of these CDs were revealed. At last, these CDs were successfully applied in LEDs. This electrochemical approach provides a promising foundation for the preparation of CDs and their subsequent applications in LEDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the past few decades, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted significant attention owing to their tunable photoluminescence (PL), good biocompatibility, robust resistance to photobleaching, and easy of functionalization [1,2]. These distinctive properties facilitate a broad spectrum of their applications in bioimaging [3], information encryption [4], light-emitting diodes (LEDs) [5], and chemical sensing [6].

Synthetic methods of CDs can generally be categorized into bottom-up and top-down approaches [3,7,8]. The bottom-up method involves the decomposition of carbonized organic small molecules through pyrolysis or the stepwise chemical melting of small aromatic compounds, whereas the top-down method typically entails the chemical or physical fragmentation of carbonaceous materials. However, most of these methods are constrained by the necessity for specialized equipment, including cumbersome processes, expensive equipment, and harsh synthesis conditions. In contrast, the electrochemical method stands out for its simplicity, low cost, and easy in producing high-purity CDs [9–11]. In 2008, Pang and his collaborators reported the pioneering use of electrochemical method to synthesize CDs, employing graphite rods as working electrodes, Pt as counter electrodes, and standard calomel electrodes (SCEs) as reference electrodes, while investigating the PL properties of the prepared CDs [12].

Ionic liquids (ILs), defined as organic salts with a melting point below 100 °C. From an electrochemical perspective, ILs possess physicochemical properties such as good ionic conductivity, thermal stability, a wide electrochemical potential window, and low vapor pressure [13]. Compared with other traditional reagents, ILs not only stabilize and effectively disperse nanoparticles but also facilitate rapid nucleation of nanoparticles simultaneously [14]. Furthermore, the structure of the ILs precursors containing heteroatoms offers a natural advantage for synthesizing CDs with excellent fluorescence properties [15]. These unique properties render ILs an exceptionally valuable solvent for electrochemical electrolytes.

As a new type of carbon nanomaterial, CDs display extensive potential applications in the LEDs field due to their low cost, high energy efficiency, good light stability, and tunable PL color. Zhang and his team synthesized fluorescent CDs with high efficiency bright red color emitting without excitation dependence by one-step microwave method using triammonium citrate, formamide, and glycerol. The quantum yields of CDs was 37.4 %, and the resulting white WLEDs demonstrate a color coordinate of (0.349, 0.345) in the CIE chart, a correlated color temperature (CCT) of 4806 K, and a color rendering index (CRI) of 88.5 [16].

Here, we present a safe, controllable and straightforward electrochemical method for the synthesizing CDs. This method breaks the constraints of traditional techniques. Although electrochemical methods have been used to prepare CDs, the distinction between cathode and anode on the preparation of CDs remains relatively unexplored. The impact of electrode materials on the fluorescence characteristics of CDs has been systematically explored for the first time. A one-step electrochemical synthesis of nitrogen-doped CDs, which possess unique fluorescence properties and chemical structures, has been successfully accomplished under mild and environmentally friendly conditions by employing graphite as the carbon source and utilizing the electrolytic effect of ionic liquids. Our research indicates that the selection of electrode materials exerts a pronounced influence on the surface structure and fluorescent properties of CDs. A profound exploration into the formation and luminescence mechanisms of these CDs has been conducted, and their practical applicability within LEDs has been successfully validated. This work provides not only a novel perspective for the electrochemical synthesis of CDs and the further exploration of their electrolytic mechanisms but also a solid foundation for the continuous advancement and innovation in the domain of carbon-based nanomaterials.

The electrolytic materials comprised graphite and Pt wire. The graphite was nuclear grade pure isostatic graphite obtained from Japan Toyo Tanso, model IG-110 with dimensions of 35 mm × 8 mm × 3 mm. The Pt wire was 99.99 % purity with a diameter of 1 mm. The IL 1-butyl-3-methylimidazole chloride salt ([BMIM]Cl) is of analytically pure. Additionally, a GaN LED chip without phosphor coating, emitting at a wavelength of 395 nm, was utilized. The epoxy resin purity: epoxy value: 0.46–0.5 mol/100 g, viscosity (25℃): 1100–1600 mPa.s. All other chemicals were of analytical grade.

A 1 M aqueous solution of the ionic liquid [BMIM]Cl served as the electrolyte. Graphite and Pt wires were vertically immersed in 40 mL of this electrolyte, maintained at a distance of 3 cm apart, and a voltage of 10 V was applied using a power supply (HY301B) for a duration of 1 hour. During the process, the electrolyte was stirred continuously by a magnetic stirrer. All experiments were conducted at room temperature.

As the reaction progressed, the solution underwent a color change from colorless to yellow, ultimately turning to brownish-yellow, accompanied by bubble formation near the electrodes. Comparative experiments were conducted to prepare CD-1, CD-2, and CD-3, using different electrode configurations: graphite as both anode and cathode, graphite as cathode with Pt wire as anode, and graphite as anode with Pt wire as cathode, respectively. Detailed experimental conditions are summarized in Table 1.

Prior to characterization and application, the obtained products were thoroughly washed with distilled water, followed by centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 15 minutes to collect the supernatant. This supernatant was then filtered through a 0.22 µm pore size filter membrane and subjected to dialysis in a dialysis bag (MWCO = 500 Da) for 48 hours to obtain a purified CDs solution via deionization. A portion of the CDs solution was freeze-dried to yield CDs powder. The schematic representation of the process is depicted in Scheme 1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and characterization of LEDs for CDs
ce:section.ce:para: LEDs were constructed utilizing LED chips that emitted light at a wavelength of 395 nm. 1 mg CDs powder was mixed with 500 µL ethanol with thorough stirring to ensure complete dissolution. Subsequently, 500 µL of epoxy resin was followed added to the mixture. Approximately 3 drops of the above derived mixture were dispensed into LED molds and dried naturally at room temperature overnight for curing. The color of the light emitted by the LEDs was assessed using a CIE (Commission Internationale de L'Eclairage 1931) calorimeter system. All measurements were conducted under dark conditions to ensure accuracy.

Absolute quantum yield (QY) measurements were conducted using a PerkinElmer FS5 spectrometer equipped with a calibrated integrating sphere. The test light from the FLS 920 spectrometer was directed onto the integrating sphere. The QY was calculated as the ratio of the number of emitted fluorescent quanta to the number of absorbed light quanta. An aqueous solution of CDs was placed in a cuvette to measure its QY, with the solvent water used as a blank sample for reference.

Absorption spectra were obtained using a SHIMADZU UV-3600 ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). PL spectra were recorded in a PerkinElmer LS-55 fluorescence spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA). Time-resolved fluorescence spectra were analyzed using a 400 nm nanosecond diode laser as an excitation source and a PerkinElmer FS5 (PerkinElmer, USA) time-corrected single photon counting system.

A 10-fold diluted solution (10 μL) of the purified CDs was drop-cast onto a copper mesh, followed by drying. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were acquired using a JEOL JEM-F200 microscope (Jeol, Japan) operated at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The size distribution of the CDs, derived from the TEM images, was analyzed using NanoMeasurer 1.2 software, ensuring that each sample's distribution was based on over 200 points from various images. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) was measured in the 400–4000 cm−1 range using a VERTEX70 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). For X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis, 30 μL of the purified and diluted CDs solution was deposited onto a silicon surface and dried at room temperature. The XPS spectra were recorded using a Thermo Scientific ESCALAB Xi+ spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, USA). Raman spectra were collected using LABRAN HR Evolution spectrometer (Horiba, Japan) with an excitation wavelength of 514 nm.

The optical properties of the CDs were characterized through the analysis of fluorescence spectra, UV–vis spectra, fluorescence decay curves, and quantum yields. The photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra of the three CDs are depicted in Fig. 1a-c. Based on their emission spectra at various excitation wavelength, both CD-1 and CD-2 showed obvious excitation dependence, influenced by the introduced CO, C-O and CN functional groups as well as the morphology heterogeneity of CDs [17]. In contrast, CD-3 showed weaker excitation dependence. As the excitation wavelength increased from 300 nm to 360 nm, the center of the emission wavelength of CD-3 remained almost constant at approximately 325 nm. With further increases in the excitation wavelength, the emission wavelength was gradually red-shifted and decrease in intensity. The concentration-dependent PL of CDs in the study of M et al. confirmed that the emission from the blue center was independent of excitation [18], and a similar phenomenon is observed here for CD-3. The excitation-independent PL property was attributed to eigen-state emission, which was predominantly influenced by the carbon core [19]. The normalized maximum emission spectra of the three CDs in the aqueous phase are presented in Fig.S1, with maximum emission wavelength of 475 nm, 472 nm, and 425 nm corresponding to excitation wavelength of 390 nm, 390 nm, and 320 nm, respectively. CD-1, with smaller particle size and more significant surface defects, played a dominant role in PL by varying the excitation wavelength [20]. Their CIE coordinates were (0.21, 0.29), (0.19, 0.27) and (0.17, 0.14), as shown in Fig. 1f. In Fig. 1a-c, the insets show the emission images of CDs in the aqueous phase, with colors consistent with the CIE coordinates, emitting cyan, blue-green and blue, respectively. Three CDs in the aqueous phase under UV light and day light are presented in Fig.S2, as well as the three CDs in the solid state. Detailed information on the excitation relevance of the CDs is provided in Table S1. Fig. 1d depicts the distinct excitation spectra of the three CDs. The excitation spectra of CD-1 and CD-2 extended from the UV region to the visible region, while the excitation spectrum of CD-3 was mainly concentrated in the UV region. Notably, both CD-1 and CD-2 had more than one excitation peak corresponding to different luminescence centers. These centers were associated with graphene cores (shorter wavelength) and surface defect bands (longer wavelength), which trapped excited state electrons and promoted radiative transitions, thus enhancing PL performance [10,21].

To delve deeper into the PL dynamics of CDs, time-resolved single photon counting measurements were conducted on the samples. Figure S3 depicts the time-resolved PL spectra of three CDs excited at a wavelength of 375 nm. The similarity observed in the fluorescence decay curves suggests a comparable PL process across all samples. These decay curves were then fitted using a double exponential function, as follows:

The fitted parameters are summarized in Table S2. It shows the mean lifetime (τ avg), including the short-lived component (τ 1) and the long-lived component (τ 2) with their relative percentage contributions, which corresponds to the composite process in the core band (π-π*) and the edge band (n-π*), respectively [22]. The χ 2 denotes the confidence factor, when 0.8 <χ 2 < 1.3, it means that the fitting results are reliably applicable. From the table, it can be seen that the average lifetimes (τ avg) of CD-1, CD-2, and CD-3 are 8.70 ns, 8.80 ns, and 6.28 ns, respectively. An interesting trend is that the contribution of the area percentage of the short-lived component τ 1 gradually increases and that of the long-lived component τ 2 gradually decreases from CD-1 to CD-3. The area percentage of the long-lived component τ 2 is much larger compared to that of the short-lived component τ 1, indicating that the edge state of CDs contributes predominantly to the fluorescence lifetime. The long-lived components τ 2 corresponding to CD-1, CD-2 and CD-3 are 10.37 ns, 10.96 ns and 8.26 ns, respectively. The percentage area of the long-lived component τ 2 decreased in the following order: 79.12 % (CD-1) > 74.00 % (CD-2) > 66.36 % (CD-3) under 375 nm excitation, which was related to the oxygen-containing and nitrogen-containing surface functional groups [23]. The absolute quantum yields (QYs) of CD-1, CD-2 and CD-3 reached 1.53 %, 1.49 % and 1.32 %, respectively (Fig. S4).

For the UV–vis spectrum, as shown in Fig. 1e, a broad absorption band existed in the range of 280–340 nm for the absorption spectra of all CDs. Absorption with a long tail indicated that CDs had a complicated bandgap system, and this band was assigned to the electron leaps of n-π* molecules or nitrogen-containing and oxygen-containing groups at the surface/edge. In addition, CD-2 and CD-3 had a distinct shoulder peak at 200–230 nm, which corresponded to the intrinsic state (π-π*) transition of the CC bond in the sp2 structural region in the carbon core [24]. Among them, CD-1 exhibited the highest absorbance at the same mass concentration due to the highest content of oxygen-containing surface functional groups and nitrogen-containing surface functional groups. In order to determine the variation of the bandgap of the three CDs, the effective optical bandgap values (E g) of each CD were calculated using the Tauc relation based on the UV–vis absorption data [25].
(αhν)n= A(hν - E g)

Where α is the absorption coefficient, hν is the photon energy, A is a constant, E g is the optical band gap, and the index n is directly related to the semiconductor type. CDs were considered direct bandgap semiconductors, so the value of n = 2 was used in this study. Fig. S5 shows the relationship between (αhν)2 and hν for the three CDs. The optical bandgap (E g) was determined by a linear fit of the (αhν)2 and hν curves. From the fitted relationship, it can be concluded that the energy difference between the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) band gaps increased from 2.89 eV for CD-1–3.45 eV for CD-3. Oxidation and nitridation disrupted the electronic network, resulting in the formation of direct electronic band gaps. The magnitude of these band gaps increased with the extent of oxidation and nitridation, thereby adjusting the HOMO and LUMO energy gaps of the materials [26,27]. Therefore, the combination of CD-1's smaller particle size and the incorporation of oxygenated/nitrogenated functional groups collectively leads to a narrower band gap compared to CD-3, ultimately causing a red-shifted PL emission for CD-1.

Optical stability is vital for luminescent nano materials. In order to verify their practicality, the CDs were performed to light stability test. Under continuous irradiation with a 365 nm UV lamp (8 W), the PL intensity of the CDs remained almost unchanged in 120 min, showing excellent PL stability (Fig. S6). Furthermore, the PL intensity of these CDs was investigated in NaCl solutions of varying concentrations, revealing a negligible change, which indicated that the PL property remained unaffected by alterations in ionic strength (Fig. S7). Consequently, the CDs exhibited outstanding optical stability and high practical applicability.

Zeta potentials of CDs were tested to characterize their surface properties and electron densities. The Zeta potentials of CD-1, CD-2, and CD-3 were −32.14 mV, −35.42 mV, and −28.67 mV (Fig. S8), respectively, indicates the presence of negatively charged functional groups on their surfaces. The large absolute values of all zeta potentials indicated sufficient stability to resist agglomeration.

Table S3 presents a comparison between our work and other relevant studies. It is apparent that the electrochemical method employed in this study for the preparation of CDs offers several advantages over other traditional methods, including mild reaction conditions, simple operation, low cost, and environmental friendliness. In the context of increasing emphasis on environmental sustainability and production efficiency, the advantages of the electrochemical method provide it with a promising development prospect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and chemical structure of CDs

ce:section.ce:para: The morphology and size distribution of these three CDs were characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). In the TEM images presented in Fig. 2a-c, it is evident that the CDs are uniformly dispersed quasi-spherical nanoparticles exhibiting excellent monodispersity. The corresponding particle size distribution curves for the three CDs are depicted in Fig. 2g-i. Specifically, CD-1 exhibits a particle size range of 1.75–5.25 nm, with an average diameter of 2.98 nm, which aligns well with the typical size of most CD-1 particles. Conversely, CD-2 displays a broader size distribution, ranging from 1.47 to 8.43 nm, with an average diameter of approximately 3.43 nm. Compared to CD-1, CD-2 exhibited a heterogeneous size distribution, with over 50 % of the particles exceeding 3.43 nm in diameter. CD-3 had an average particle size of 5.92 nm. CD-1, characterized by a narrower size distribution, displayed a longer wavelength in PL, whereas CD-3, with a broader size distribution, exhibited a shorter wavelength PL. This phenomenon does not conform to the quantum size effect, which is attributed to the influence of the surface states, chemical groups, and structural defects of the CDs [28,29]. Compared to CD-2 and CD-3, CD-1 possesses more nitrogen-containing and oxygen-containing functional groups, which generate surface states. When interactions occur between these surface states and the intrinsic states of the CDs, the characteristics of the electron transitions was altered [30,31]. Moreover, as shown in Fig. S9, EPR results reveal that CD-1 has more carbon vacancies, which can serve as the electron trapping center, resulting in the redshift of the emission wavelength [32]. The crystalline nature of the CDs was investigated by high-resolution TEM (HRTEM). The HRTEM images show graphite lattice fringes with lattice spacing of 0.19 nm, 0.21 nm, 0.23 nm, and 0.24 nm (Fig. 2d-f), corresponding to the (104), (100), (112), and (002) planes of graphite, respectively [33,34]. This finding indicates the presence of highly crystalline graphite structures within the CDs.

The surface groups and chemical composition of CDs were characterized using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As shown in Fig. S10, the FTIR spectra of various CDs exhibit several identical vibrational bands with similar shapes and peak positions, indicating their similarity in chemical composition and structure. The characteristic peaks observed at 3450 cm −1 and 1460 cm −1 are attributed to the stretching vibration and in-plane bending vibration of -OH, respectively [35].The peak at 1627 cm −1 indicates the presence of CO [36], confirming the abundance of -COOH functional groups on the surface of the prepared CDs. Additionally, the bands at 2956 cm−1 and 2871 cm−1 correlate with symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibrations of the C-H bond in the alkyl group of the imidazole cation BMIM+, respectively, affirming the binding between CDs and BMIM+ [37,38]. Furthermore, peaks corresponding to -NH stretching (754 cm⁻¹) and –NH2 stretching (1168 cm⁻¹) are observed. Among all peaks, the most prominent and intense is the characteristic absorption peak of the hydroxyl group. The peaks corresponding to oxygen-containing groups and other functional groups in the spectra indicate the successful oxidation of graphite and formation of functional groups. Due to the abundant polar function groups, these CDs exhibit excellent solubility in polar solvents, such as water and ethanol [39,40].

XPS analysis was employed to further investigate the chemical composition and structural characteristics of the prepared CDs. The XPS spectra of the samples are presented in Fig. 3. The full XPS spectrum exhibits peaks around 285 eV, 400 eV, 532 eV, and 198 eV, corresponding to C 1 s, N 1 s, O 1 s, and Cl 2p, respectively, confirming the presence of carbon (C), nitrogen (N), oxygen (O), and chlorine (Cl) in all the prepared CDs.

Table S4 summarizes the elemental composition percentages and their ratios for the three CDs. A sequential increase in the C/O and C/N atomic ratios is observed from CD-1 to CD-3, indicating a gradual reduction in the oxygen-containing and nitrogen-containing functional groups on the CD surfaces. Specifically, CD-1 exhibits a higher proportion of oxidized and nitrogenated surface states compared to CD-2 and CD-3. The increase in oxidation and nitridation results in an increase in surface defects, which can trap electrons, thereby narrowing the energy gap between the HOMO and LUMO [41]. As shown in Figure S5 above, CD-1 exhibits the narrowest band gap of 2.89 eV. Such narrowing of the energy gap decelerates the exciton decay via non-radiative pathways, effectively prolonging the lifetime of CDs. Furthermore, the incorporation of nitrogen atoms can generate new energy levels, which act as additional luminous centers and further extend the fluorescence lifetime of CDs [42]. Notably, the trend in C/O content and C/N content align with the decreasing trend in the area percentage of the long-lived component (τ 2) from CD-1 to CD-3. This reaffirms the correlation between the long-lived component (τ 2) and the oxygen-containing functional groups along with the nitrogen-containing functional groups [23]. Therefore, these functional groups play a pivotal role in enhancing the percentage of the long-lived component and extending the fluorescence lifetime.

The deconvoluted high-resolution C1s, N1s, and O1s spectra of CD-1, CD-2, and CD-3 were also depicted, with the results summarized in Table S5. The high-resolution spectra of C1s contains three peaks at 284.8 eV, 286.1 eV, and 287.4 eV, which are attributed to CC/C-C, C-N, and CO/CN, respectively [43–45]. The N1s band analysis reveals three peaks, pyridine nitrogen, pyrrole nitrogen and graphitic nitrogen at binding energies of 399.5 eV, 400.7 eV, and 401.4 eV, respectively [46]. In the high-resolution O1s spectrum, two peaks located at 531.5 and 532.7 eV are assigned to CO and C-OH groups, respectively [47].

The above results demonstrate the abundant presence of -OH and CO groups on the surface of CDs, which may be relevant to the combination of suspended carbon bonds on the surface of the exfoliated carbon fragments and hydroxyl radicals generated during water decomposition [48,49]. The formation of these oxygen-containing groups and other functional groups indicates the successful oxidation and functionalization of graphite, thereby enhancing the water solubility of the CDs [50,51].

In Raman spectroscopy, the peaks located at 1350 cm−1 and 1580 cm−1 mimic disordered structures or defects (D band) and the graphitic domain (G band), respectively, corresponding to sp2 and sp3 carbon configurations. The G band is associated with the vibrations modes of carbon atoms bonded with sp2 in a two-dimensional hexagonal lattice, corresponding to the E 2 g mode of graphite. Conversely, the D band, centered around 1360 cm⁻¹ , is related to the vibrations of carbon atoms with dangling bonds present in the termination plane of disordered graphite or glassy carbon [52]. The intensity ratio of D-band to G-band (I D/I G) serves as a quantitative measure of defects within the graphene matrix [53]. As illustrated in Fig. S11, the I D/I G values for CD-1, CD-2, and CD-3 were 1.08, 1.01, and 0.91, respectively. This result is consistent with that obtained by EPR in Fig. S9, CD-1 had more structural defects than CD-2 and CD-3. Notably, these values align with the observed trend in the area percentage of τ 2. The higher I D/I G ratio of CD-1 suggested that CD-1 had a larger sp2 structure domain due to more structural defects introduced by the functional groups compared to those in the other CDs, which could contribute to a redshift of the PL [54]. In summary, the Raman spectra of the three CDs confirm their high degree of graphitization, and the presence of graphitic crystal regions is further validated by HRTEM analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of the formation of CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
When graphite is used as the cathode and anode materials, the electrochemical stripping process in the electrolytic system can be divided into the following three main steps:
(1)
Directional movement of anions and cations and formation of free radicals. When the voltage applied to the electrode exceeds the narrow electrochemical potential window of water, the distilled water in the electrolyte first inevitably dissociates into hydroxyl and oxygen radicals [55,56], while H2 and O2 are produced near the cathode and anode, respectively, and released from the electrode in the form of bubbles, and OH- and H+ appear in the electrolyte [57–59]. At the same time, imidazole ions [BMIM]+ in the IL gain free electrons, move toward the cathode and are reduced to imidazole radicals.[60]
It is worth noting that due to the lower oxidation potential of Cl- compared to water, the oxidation of Cl- may occurs preferentially at the anode, followed by the anodic oxidation of water, which in turn generates hydroxyl and oxygen radicals [55,56].
(2)
Impact processes of anions and cations. The various anions and cations produced move toward the electrode, continuously attacking and eroding the electrode, promoting graphite fragmentation and the initial opening of sites and boundary defects in the graphite [50].
(3)
Formation of surface groups and intercalation stripping process. Reactive radicals further break graphite bonds, invade graphite from edge positions, grain boundaries, or surface defects of graphite, and generate new, stable bonds, such as C-OH and C-O. The formation of new surface groups disrupts the original bond-valent structure, causing the original graphite structure to split and the layer spacing to widen, resulting in the exfoliation to form carbon nanoparticles, namely CDs. Specifically, the oxidation of the edge planes of the graphite anode promotes the embedding of the anions, which leads to the depolarization and expansion of the graphite anode [61]. In the vicinity of the cathode, larger imidazolium radicals can be inserted into the graphite planes of the graphite that have been disrupted by hydroxyl radicals into the π bonds, thus promoting the binding of BMIM+ and CDs (π-π or cation-π) [62].

The stirring action of the magnets is accompanied throughout the electrolysis system. The stirring of the magnets rotates the anions, cations, and various reactive radicals produced during the electrolysis process, which facilitates impaction and further promote the formation of CDs.

Based on the electrochemical stripping process outlined above, the electrolytic systems designed for synthesizing CD-2 and CD-1 exhibit distinct anode behaviors due to the use of Pt wire instead of graphite as the anode material. Specifically, in the system for CD-2, neither Cl⁻ oxidation nor anodic water oxidation occurs at the Pt anode. Conversely, in the system for CD-1, Cl⁻ oxidation precedes anodic water oxidation near the anode, as previously mentioned. Notably, the degree of graphite stripping and cleavage is significantly less pronounced near the anode compared to the cathode in both systems. Consequently, the similar cathode stripping processes lead to comparable optimal excitation/emission wavelengths for CD-1 and CD-2, approximately 390/475 nm.

In contrast, the electrolytic system for synthesizing CD-3 employs a Pt wire cathode, preventing the hydroxylation, oxidation of graphite, and intercalation of imidazole radicals that typically occur at the graphite electrode. This results in weaker intercalation of BMIM⁺ and minimal hydroxylation and oxidation of CD-3. XPS full-spectrum analysis reveals that CD-3 contains less N and O elements compared to CD-1 and CD-2, leading to notably different fluorescence properties between CD-1 and CD-3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The application of CDs in LED lamps
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5 shows the solid-state PL characteristics of LEDs fabricated using the three CDs. Utilizing a 395 nm ultraviolet (UV) light-emitting chip as the base, the LEDs, after being coated with a mixture of CDs and epoxy resin, emitted green, blue-green, and purple light, as depicted in Fig. 5a-c, respectively. Figure S12 shows the illumination of the individual UV chip. The corresponding emission spectra of these three LEDs are presented in Fig. 5d-f, with dominant wavelength distributions centered at 507 nm, 489 nm, and 458 nm, respectively. These values exhibit a red-shift compared to the emission wavelength of the CD solutions, which can be attributed to the concentration-dependent emission property of CDs [63]. The color rendering indexes (CRIs) of the LEDs were measured to be 76.2, 75.1, and 78.7, respectively. The color gamut coordinates of these LEDs were calculated using CIE 1931 software, yielding coordinates of (0.29, 0.38), (0.24, 0.33), and (0.22, 0.15) for Fig. 5g-i, respectively. The color temperatures CCT of these LEDs are 7150 K, 7385 K, and 10258 K. The above results demonstrate that the LEDs exhibit excellent optical properties, indicating the feasibility of using the prepared CDs as replacements for traditional fluorescent materials in LED lamp applications.

In summary, three CDs with distinct fluorescence properties and chemical structures have been successfully synthesized through a one-step electrochemical synthetic method. These CDs possess crystalline graphite structures with distinct two-dimensional lattice fringes and exhibit surfaces rich in oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functional groups. In the electrochemical system where graphite serves as the carbon source and ILs as the electrolyte, processes including ion impact, radical intercalation, hydroxylation, and oxidation are intimately associated with the formation of CDs from graphite. Importantly, it has been discovered that the types of electrode materials play a pivotal role in determining the surface structures and fluorescent properties of the CDs, thereby directly influencing their fluorescence properties. CDs prepared with graphite as both the anode and cathode exhibit superior fluorescence properties, which show the optimal excitation/emission wavelength (390/475 nm) for a redshift compared to CDs (320/425 nm) prepared with graphite as anode and platinum as cathode. Moreover, the successful demonstration of diverse luminescent LEDs using the unique photoluminescence properties of these CDs showcases their practical applicability and potential for future optoelectronic devices. This work not only provides valuable guidance for the electrochemical preparation of CDs, but also lays a foundation for further exploration and innovation in the field of carbon-based nanomaterials, holding potential significance for various optoelectronic applications.
